english: 1. to spread, to gain ground, esp. of a fire frq.; also fig.: bdág-gi .dod-čhags-kyi me mčéd-pas (bdag gi 'dod chags kyi me mched pas) as the fire of voluptuousness spread or increased within me Dzl.; also in the following sense: már-me γčìg-la γčìg mčéd ltar (mar me gcig la gcig mched ltar) as one kindles one light by another Mil.; γčìg gleṅ γnyis gleṅ rím-pas mčéd-de (gcig gleng gnyis gleng rim pas mched de) as (the news) spread more and more by gossiping people Pth. -- skye-mčéd (skye mched) v. skye (skye). -- 2. = γyó-ba (gyo ba), mi-mčéd-paí dád-pa (mi mched pa'i dad pa) = dád-pa brtán-po (dad pa brtan po).

the best, the most excellent in its kind, skyés-bu mčóg (skyes bu mchog), mií mčóg (mi'i mchog), rkaṅ-γnyís-rnams-kyi mčóg (rkang gnyis rnams kyi mchog) Buddha; nyes-ltúṅ-gis ma póg-pa (nyes ltung gis ma phog pa) (or na (na)) mčóg yín-te póg-rtíṅ bšágs-pa byéd-pa rab yín (mchog yin te phog rting bshags pa byed pa rab yin) Mil. the best thing is, not to have been surprised by sin, but after having been surprised, it is the best to confess it (and thus to atone for it); γsuṅ-mčóg (gsung mchog) chief or fundamental doctrine, main dogma, principal commandment etc. Glr.; ṅa ni .jíg-rtén .dí-na mčóg (nga ni 'jig rten 'di na mchog) I am the highest in the world (says Buddha immediately after his birth) Glr.; γnas-mčóg (gnas mchog) the most glorious or splendid country Glr.; ro-mčóg (ro mchog) excellent taste or flavour Mil.; mǩas-mčóg-rnams (mkhas mchog rnams) most learned gentlemen Zam.; also as a complimentary word; mi mčóg ǩyod (mi mchog khyod) most honoured Sir! Pth.; mčóg-dmán (mchog dman), mčóg daṅ tun-móṅ (mchog dang thun mong), mčóg daṅ pál-pa (mchog dang phal pa), good and bad, first-rate and common, fine and ordinary, of goods etc.; eminent and ordinary, of mental gifts, talents etc.; mčóg-tu γyúr-pa (mchog tu gyur pa) = mčóg (mchog), e.g. mi-rnams-kyi náṅ-na mčóg-tu γyúr-pa γčìg (mi rnams kyi nang na mchog tu gyur pa gcig) one that has risen among men, so as to become their chief Glr.; yúl-rnams-kyi mčóg-tu γyúr-pa (yul rnams kyi mchog tu gyur pa) the most splendid of countries. -- Adv.: mčóg-tu (mchog tu) very, most, with verbs: bón-po-la mčóg-tu mós-pa žig (bon po la mchog tu mos pa zhig) a great admirer of the Bonpos Mil.; gen. with adjectives: ro mčóg-tu mṅár-ba (ro mchog tu mngar ba) extremely sweet; with the comparative: much, far, by far, greatly, dé-bas mčóg-tu čheo (de bas mchog tu che'o) ... is far or much greater than that Dzl. Comp. mčóg-sbyín pyag-rgyá (mchog sbyin phyag rgya) a gesture made in practising magic, in conjuring up or exorcising ghosts. -- mčóg-zúṅ (mchog zung) the model pair, the two most excellent amongst Buddha's disciples, Shariibú and Maudgalgyibú, v. Köpp. -- mčóg-ríṅ (mchog ring) longest Thgy.
to leap, to jump, frq., e.g. čhur (chur) into the water; mi-seb-la (mi seb la) among the people, e.g. of a mad dog).

I. vb. 1. to honour, revere, respect, receive with honour, kún-gyis bkúr žiṅ mčód-paí .os (kun gyis bkur zhing mchod pa'i 'os) worthy of being honoured and praised by all; usually ccapir. (rarely dp.) in the special sense: to honour saints or deities by offering articles of food, flowers, music, the sound, odour and flavour of which they are supposed to relish, hence to treat, entertain, regale (the gods), and in a more general sense applied also to lifeless objects, e.g. to honour a sepulchre in such a manner; Glr. mčód-pa (mchod pa) may therefore in English be sometimes translated by: to offer, to sacrifice, but it should always be borne in mind, that no idea of self-denial or yielding up a precious good (as is implied by the English word), or of slaughtering, as in the Greek θύειν, can be connected with the Tibetan word itself, though in practice bloody sacrifices, abhorred as they are by pure Buddhism in theory, are not quite unheard of, not only animals being immolated to certain deities, but also men notoriously noxious to religion slaughtered as dmar-mčód (dmar mchod), red offering, to the dgrá-lha (dgra lha) q.v. -- 2. C. resp. to eat, drink, take, taste, (in W. expressed by *dón-če* (*don ce*)). II. sbst. offering, oblation, libation, mčód-pa .búl-ba (mchod pa 'bul ba), W. *púl-če* (*phul ce*) frq., also byéd-pa (byed pa); ról-mo mčód-par .búl-ba (rol mo mchod par 'bul ba) to bring an
